ratings.plot: In a corrupt city, a small-time gangster and the estranged wife of a pot dealer find themselves thrown together in an escapade of love, money, drugs and danger.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of Malle's finest achievements and a defining performance from Burt Lancaster. The film captures a decaying city in transition with the precision of a master filmmaker at his peak.

ratings.plot: Autumn Moon (Sam Lee), a low-rent triad living in Hong Kong, struggles to find meaning in his hopelessly violent existence.

verdict.reason: One of the essential films of Hong Kong's handover period that deserves a big screen viewing. Chan shoots with handheld 16mm that captures the raw energy of street-level desperation better than any digital transfer.

ratings.plot: Inside a Hong Kong prison, two inmates form a friendship and face the difficulty of life on the inside.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone serious about Hong Kong cinema at its peak. Ringo Lam's direction strips away melodrama to focus on raw human connection inside brutal institutional walls.

ratings.plot: Eight-year-old Ahmed has mistakenly taken his friend Mohammad's notebook. He wants to return it, or else his friend will be expelled from school. The boy determinedly sets out to find Mohammad's home in the neighbouring village.

verdict.reason: Kiarostami's breakthrough transforms a simple premise into profound cinema. The Iranian landscape and natural light create images that lose impact on small screens.

Essential viewing for Jia Zhangke's breakthrough debut that launched sixth-generation Chinese cinema. The handheld DV photography captures working-class displacement with an intimacy that established his entire aesthetic approach.

ratings.plot: Several people try to talk 27-year-old Noriko into marrying, but all she wants is to keep on caring for her widowed father.

verdict.reason: Essential Ozu at his peak, capturing family dynamics with unmatched precision. The tatami mat camera angles and seasonal imagery create a viewing experience that demands the big screen.

ratings.plot: A truck driver stops at a small family-run noodle shop and decides to help its fledgling business. The story is intertwined with various vignettes about the relationship of love and food.

verdict.reason: See Itami's ramen western on the biggest screen you can find. The film's obsessive food cinematography and precise comic timing demand theatrical viewing.

ratings.plot: A young woman joins a charming con man on the run, leaving a trail of crime and passion as they hustle through the Southeast in search of her estranged mother.

verdict.reason: Rehmeier delivers a rare perfect crime romance that justifies the theatrical experience. The Southeast setting gives the con game a regional authenticity that separates it from generic grifter movies.

ratings.plot: Two neighbors, a persecuted journalist and a resigned housewife, meet during Hitler's visit to Italy in May 1938.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of the great performances in Mastroianni's career opposite Loren in peak form. Scola constructs the entire film around a single apartment building, creating intimacy that would be impossible to replicate at home.

ratings.plot: A anarchist leader (Fulvio) wishes to retire, as he is old and tired. He tries to hide himself, but his friends find him and insist he carries on helping them.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of the Taviani brothers' most complex political dramas. The film's structure mirrors its protagonist's fractured psyche through deliberate pacing that rewards patient viewers.

ratings.plot: During the Nazi occupation of Rome in 1944, the Resistance leader, Giorgio Manfredi, is chased by the Nazis as he seeks refuge and a way to escape.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's most important works about wartime resistance. Rossellini shot on location in bombed-out Rome with nonprofessional actors, creating an immediacy that no studio recreation has matched.

ratings.plot: Italy, 1900. Agata is a young woman who embarks on a desperate journey to reach a mysterious sanctuary to save her daughter's soul from the eternal damnation of Limbo.

verdict.reason: This Italian debut delivers one of the year's most striking visions of maternal desperation. Samani shoots the Friulian landscape like a primordial dreamscape that makes the spiritual quest feel genuinely otherworldly.
Worth seeking out this rare gem that earned universal critical praise. The film's intimate scale and precise character work make it ideal for theatrical viewing.

ratings.plot: The Prince of Salina, a noble aristocrat of impeccable integrity, tries to preserve his family and class amid the tumultuous social upheavals of 1860s Sicily.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Visconti's operatic vision of aristocratic decline shot in gorgeous Technicolor. The three-hour runtime lets you sink into the ballroom sequence that ranks among cinema's greatest extended scenes.

ratings.plot: A dozen British soldiers, lost in a Mesopotamian desert during World War I, are menaced by unseen Arab enemies.

verdict.reason: Essential Ford from his prime period, building unbearable tension through what you never see. The desert cinematography and sound design create dread that modern war films still chase.

ratings.plot: Follow the history of misdeeds and debauchery at Walt Disney World Resort over 30 years, which leads to the theft of almost half a million dollars worth of stolen props.

verdict.reason: This focused documentary delivers exactly what its title promises without padding the runtime. Bailey keeps the 74-minute structure tight enough to maintain momentum while covering three decades of corporate malfeasance.

ratings.plot: A seemingly timid but secretly ruthless ingénue insinuates herself into the lives of an aging Broadway star and her circle of theater friends.

verdict.reason: Essential theatrical experience for one of cinema's sharpest scripts. Bette Davis delivers her most commanding performance as the aging Broadway star who meets her match.

ratings.plot: Writer James Baldwin tells the story of race in modern America with his unfinished novel, Remember This House.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing that builds a devastating argument through Baldwin's own words and archival footage. Peck lets the writer's voice carry the film without contemporary talking heads diluting the power.

ratings.plot: Paddington, now happily settled with the Brown family and a popular member of the local community, picks up a series of odd jobs to buy the perfect present for his Aunt Lucy's 100th birthday, only for the gift to be stolen.

verdict.reason: This is as close to perfect family filmmaking as you can get. Paul King turns every frame into a handcrafted dollhouse where even the prison sequence feels like a warm hug.

ratings.plot: Naples faces dual volcanic threats from Vesuvius and Campi Flegrei. Amid increasing tremors, archaeologists work as residents live anxiously, haunted by Pompeii's fate while emergency services strain.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Rosi's masterful ability to find profound human stories in environments of quiet crisis. The film captures the eerie beauty of living under constant geological threat with his signature observational style that made Fire at Sea unforgettable.

ratings.plot: Tales told from the perspective of a lonely old tree standing in the middle of a botanical garden.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Enyedi's return after her Golden Bear triumph with On Body and Soul. The 147-minute runtime lets her poetic vision breathe without rushing the tree's centuries-spanning observations.

ratings.plot: A weak-willed Italian man becomes a fascist flunky who goes abroad to arrange the assassination of his old teacher, now a political dissident.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's greatest achievements in visual storytelling. Bertolucci and cinematographer Vittorio Storaro create compositions that turn fascist psychology into pure cinematic architecture.

ratings.plot: When her niece is found dead along with three friends after viewing a supposedly cursed videotape, reporter Reiko sets out to investigate. She finds the tape, watches it and receives a phone call informing her that she'll die in a...

verdict.reason: Essential horror that invented the template every modern ghost story copies. Nakata builds dread through sound design and negative space rather than jump scares.

movies.title: Marcel the Shell with Shoes On

ratings.plot: A cash-strapped documentary maker decides to make his newest documentary about a mollusk shell he finds living in his Airbnb with his friends.

verdict.reason: This deserves the biggest screen you can find for a film that transforms intimate stop-motion into genuine magic. Fleischer Camp's direction makes Marcel's tiny world feel vast and emotionally enormous.

ratings.plot: The relationship between a father and daughter is complicated by the arrival of a handsome young man.

verdict.reason: Denis delivers one of her finest films about family bonds dissolving and reforming. Her camera finds poetry in the smallest gestures between characters who rarely say what they mean.

movies.title: Portrait of a Lady on Fire

ratings.plot: On an isolated island in Brittany at the end of the eighteenth century, a female painter is obliged to paint a wedding portrait of a young woman.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone who believes cinema can capture the intensity of unspoken desire. Sciamma builds every frame around the act of looking, making the theater the perfect place to experience her controlled visual intimacy.

ratings.plot: A family of six settles into their new home on Vancouver Island as internal dynamics are slowly revealed through the eyes of the youngest child.

verdict.reason: See this intimate family portrait while it has theatrical momentum. Romvari shoots through a child's perspective with uncommon sensitivity to how kids actually process adult tensions.

ratings.plot: A young woman separated from her lover by war faces a life-altering decision.

verdict.reason: Essential cinema that demands the big screen treatment for its saturated Technicolor splendor. Demy's wall-to-wall sung dialogue creates a hypnotic rhythm that theatrical sound systems deliver with proper impact.

ratings.plot: Cat is a solitary animal, but as its home is devastated by a great flood, he finds refuge on a boat populated by various species, and will have to team up with them despite their differences.

verdict.reason: Rare wordless storytelling that succeeds entirely through visual language and deserves the largest screen possible. Zilbalodis crafts each frame with painterly precision that streaming compression would diminish.

ratings.plot: A psychological thriller exploring the fragile boundary between perception and reality, where characters confront their inner demons and moral conflicts while navigating suspense-driven events that challenge identity, truth, and h...

verdict.reason: Worth the trip for a thriller that earns its 145-minute runtime with genuine psychological complexity. Shahid builds tension through character study rather than cheap scares, making this a rare thoughtful entry in a disposable genre.

ratings.plot: A family moves into a new neighborhood, and a 10-year-old named Laure deliberately presents as a boy named Mikhael to the neighborhood children.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Sciamma's precise direction that finds profound weight in small gestures and glances. The 82-minute runtime never wastes a moment, building toward an ending that trusts the audience to understand everything without explanation.

ratings.plot: This Hong Kong-set crime drama follows the lives of a hitman, hoping to get out of the business, and his elusive female partner.

verdict.reason: Wong Kar-wai's neon-soaked Hong Kong comes alive on a big screen where you can catch every detail of his restless camera. The film's kinetic energy gets lost on smaller formats.

ratings.plot: They talk about the beautiful game, but for Laurentiu Ginghina, it's not enough. Football must be modified, streamlined, freed from restraints; corners are to be rounded off, players assigned to zones and subteams, norms revised. ...

verdict.reason: Worth the trip for one of cinema's most quietly absurd philosophical comedies. Porumboiu turns a man's obsession with redesigning football into a deadpan masterpiece about Romanian bureaucratic thinking.

ratings.plot: Sophie reflects on the shared joy and private melancholy of a holiday she took with her father twenty years earlier. Memories real and imagined fill the gaps between as she tries to reconcile the father she knew with the man she d...

verdict.reason: Wells constructs a devastating memory piece that builds to an emotional knockout punch. The film's careful modulation of tone and time requires theatrical focus to land properly.

ratings.plot: Werner Herzog gains exclusive access to film inside the Chauvet caves of Southern France and captures the oldest known pictorial creations of humanity.

verdict.reason: Herzog's access to humanity's earliest cave paintings creates genuinely unrepeatable cinema. The 3D format adds archaeological depth that home viewing cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: The behind the scenes of a national drag queen contest in New York City, including the rehearsals leading up to the contest, the conversations in the dressing room and the jealousies that emerge before and after the competition.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in queer cinema history or documentary filmmaking. Simon's fly-on-the-wall approach captures raw moments of vulnerability and competition that feel decades ahead of their time.

ratings.plot: A young F.B.I. cadet must receive the help of an incarcerated and manipulative cannibal killer to help catch another serial killer, a madman who skins his victims.

verdict.reason: Essential theatrical experience that remains one of the finest psychological thrillers ever made. Demme's intimate close-ups and sound design create unbearable tension that demands the big screen.

ratings.plot: An Icelandic writer preserves vanishing glaciers, departed grandparents, family memories, and flowing time in his personal archives, transforming loss into a time capsule.

verdict.reason: Sara Dosa delivers another visually stunning meditation that demands the big screen treatment. The Icelandic glacier cinematography creates an immersive experience that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: The struggle of women in a country that excludes them from entering stadiums.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's great acts of defiance disguised as sports comedy. Panahi shot this guerrilla-style outside Tehran's Azadi Stadium during an actual World Cup qualifier, turning real risk into electric filmmaking.

ratings.plot: Henry Graham lives the life of a playboy. When his lawyer tells him that his lifestyle has consumed all his funds, Henry must avoid climbing down the social ladder. He plans to marry wealthy scientist Henrietta Lowell - and kill her.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for May's brilliant directorial debut, which she also wrote and starred in with perfect misanthropic timing. Her chemistry with Walter Matthau creates one of the sharpest dark comedies of the 1970s.

ratings.plot: The story of the Finzi-Continis, a noble family of Ferrara, during the Jewish persecution in Italy's 1930s.

verdict.reason: De Sica's final masterpiece captures the end of an era with devastating understatement. The film's elegant restraint in depicting fascism's rise makes every gesture count.

ratings.plot: After helping a wounded gang member, a strong-willed female saloon owner is wrongly suspected of murder and bank robbery by a lynch mob.

verdict.reason: Essential Ray on a big screen where his CinemaScope compositions and Trucolor saturation can overwhelm you properly. Joan Crawford's volcanic performance against Sterling Hayden's quiet masculinity creates the most psychosexual tension in any Western.

ratings.plot: In 1936, archaeologist Indiana Jones is tasked by Army Intelligence to help locate a legendary ancient power, the Ark of Covenant, before the Nazis get it first.

verdict.reason: Essential theatrical experience that demands the biggest screen you can find. Spielberg's practical stunts and Douglas Slocombe's cinematography lose their visceral impact at home.

ratings.plot: A talented piano tuner's meticulous skills for tuning pianos lead him to discover an unexpected aptitude for cracking safes, turning his life upside down.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing for a crime story that actually earns its musical hook instead of forcing it. Roher builds real tension around the specific craft of piano tuning in ways that make the safe-cracking connection feel inevitable rather than gimmicky.

ratings.plot: A science teacher wakes up alone on a spaceship. As his memory returns, he uncovers a mission to stop a mysterious substance killing Earth's sun, and realizes that an unexpected friendship may be the key.

verdict.reason: Lord and Miller bring their inventive visual storytelling to hard science fiction in a way that demands the big screen experience. The 156-minute runtime lets them build both the cosmic stakes and the unlikely friendship that anchors the story.

ratings.plot: N/A

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of cinema's greatest action sequences. Miller's practical effects and stunt work remain unmatched decades later.

ratings.plot: Marty McFly, a 17-year-old high school student, is accidentally sent 30 years into the past in a time-traveling DeLorean invented by his close friend, the maverick scientist Doc Brown.

verdict.reason: Essential big-screen experience that showcases one of the great high-concept screenplays in Hollywood history. Zemeckis orchestrates the time-travel mechanics with clockwork precision that rewards theatrical attention to detail.

ratings.plot: Cleo, a singer and hypochondriac, becomes increasingly worried that she might have cancer while awaiting test results from her doctor.

verdict.reason: Essential Varda that captures real-time anxiety better than almost any film ever made. The 90-minute runtime unfolds in near real-time as Cléo wanders Paris, creating an intimacy that demands theatrical focus.

ratings.plot: After Carlos - a 12-year-old whose father has died in the Spanish Civil War - arrives at an ominous boys' orphanage, he discovers the school is haunted and has many dark secrets which he must uncover.

verdict.reason: Del Toro's ghost story works as both supernatural thriller and Spanish Civil War allegory. The practical effects and atmospheric cinematography create genuine dread that demands theatrical darkness.

ratings.plot: In 2075, a girl witnesses a mysterious boy in a rainbow suit fall from the sky. He comes from an idyllic far future where time travel is possible. She shelters him and will do whatever it takes to help him return to his time.

verdict.reason: This animated time-travel story delivers visual invention that justifies the theatrical experience. Director Ugo Bienvenu crafts a rainbow-suited aesthetic that demands the biggest screen you can find.

ratings.plot: A fiercely independent cowboy gets himself locked up in prison to escape with an old friend.

verdict.reason: This is essential viewing for anyone who thinks they understand the American Western. Walter Matthau delivers one of his finest dramatic performances as the pragmatic sheriff caught between duty and admiration.

ratings.plot: A matador is wounded by a bull and can no longer kill in the arena. It is just one of the problems of one of the characters that unexpectedly collide in this film.

verdict.reason: Essential early Almodóvar that shows his visual style crystallizing before he became internationally famous. The bullfighting imagery creates a sexual and violent mythology that only works on a big screen.

ratings.plot: The story of a hitman for the drug cartels in Ciudad Juarez, Mexico.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of the most chilling documentaries ever made about cartel violence. Rosi's decision to keep his subject anonymous and shoot entirely in a motel room creates an intimacy that makes the horror feel immediate.

ratings.plot: During her Mallorca summer, Antonia recognizes similarities with her late grandma. She develops an influence over her mourning grandpa through dress-up, blurring who inhabits who.

verdict.reason: A striking debut that earns its place among the year's most unsettling character studies. Iglesias builds genuine psychological dread through the simple act of costume changes.

ratings.plot: A group of shoplifters take aim at a cutthroat fashion maven by stealing her clothes and reselling them at a lower price, what they call "fashion-forward philanthropy."

verdict.reason: Riley delivers his sharpest social satire yet with perfect tonal control. The fashion industry backdrop gives him fresh material to skewer capitalism with his signature wit.

ratings.plot: A girl gifted with a keen intellect and psychic powers uses both to get even with her callous family and free her kindly schoolteacher from the tyrannical grip of a sadistic headmistress.

verdict.reason: One of the best family films ever made that works perfectly for adults watching alone. DeVito's direction balances Dahl's darkness with genuine warmth in ways that most adaptations fumble.

ratings.plot: The romantic tale of a sheltered uptown Cocker Spaniel dog and a streetwise downtown Mutt.
ratings.director: Clyde Geronimi, Wilfred Jackson, Hamilton Luske

verdict.reason: One of Disney's finest hand-drawn features deserves the big screen treatment. The spaghetti scene alone justifies the theatrical experience, but the entire film showcases the studio's mastery of character animation and storytelling economy.

ratings.plot: An unceasingly kind Italian peasant and his family are blatantly exploited by a tobacco baroness.

verdict.reason: Rohrwacher creates one of cinema's most unsettling shifts from pastoral realism to modern fable. The film's jarring temporal leap transforms what begins as social drama into something far stranger and more haunting.

ratings.plot: The story of Mussolini's secret lover, Ida Dalser, and their son Albino.

verdict.reason: Bellocchio delivers one of his strongest films with fierce political urgency and theatrical power. Giovanna Mezzogiorno gives a volcanic performance that demands the big screen.

ratings.plot: An idealistic porn producer aspires to elevate his craft to an art when he discovers a hot young talent.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of Anderson's finest ensemble pieces about American dreams and delusions. The 155-minute runtime lets the story breathe across decades while maintaining perfect pacing throughout.

ratings.plot: A world-renowned K-Pop girl group balance their lives in the spotlight with their secret identities as demon hunters.

verdict.reason: This animated action-adventure earns its ticket with inventive worldbuilding that makes dual identities feel genuinely integrated rather than gimmicky. Appelhans and Kang craft fight sequences that pulse with the same energy as a live concert performance.

ratings.plot: A nurse is put in charge of a mute actress and finds that their personae are melding together.

verdict.reason: Essential Bergman at his most psychologically piercing and formally inventive. The film breaks the fourth wall with shocking meta-theatrical moments that work only in a theater with other people.

ratings.plot: An upper-class widow falls in love with a much younger, down-to-earth nurseryman, much to the disapproval of her children and criticism of her country club peers.

verdict.reason: Essential Sirk melodrama that defines the form with visual perfection and emotional complexity. The Technicolor cinematography transforms suburban conformity into a lush prison of mirrors and windows.

ratings.plot: In the slums of Rio, two kids' paths diverge as one struggles to become a photographer and the other a kingpin.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing that belongs on a big screen where Meirelles' kinetic camerawork can fully immerse you. The film's breakneck editing and saturated colors create a visual rush that streaming cannot match.

ratings.plot: Ben Sanderson, a Hollywood screenwriter who lost everything because of his alcoholism, arrives in Las Vegas to drink himself to death. There, he meets and forms an uneasy friendship and non-interference pact with prostitute Sera.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Cage's devastating career-best performance and Figgis's unflinching direction. The film's raw intimacy between two broken souls creates genuine emotional weight that demands theatrical attention.

ratings.plot: A young man hears a chance phone call telling him that a nuclear war has started and missiles will hit the city within 70 minutes.

verdict.reason: This is essential late-night paranoia viewing that builds genuine dread in real time. De Jarnatt sustains the tension for 87 minutes straight without a single wasted scene.

ratings.plot: After finding an old rifle, a young boy joins the Soviet resistance movement against ruthless German forces and experiences the horrors of World War II.

verdict.reason: Klimov's war nightmare must be experienced theatrically for maximum impact. The sound design alone justifies leaving your apartment.

ratings.plot: A cyborg from the future, identical to the one who failed to kill Sarah Connor, must now protect her ten-year-old son John from an even more advanced and powerful cyborg.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing on the biggest screen you can find. Cameron's practical effects work remains unmatched three decades later, and the T-1000's liquid metal transformations demand theatrical scale.

ratings.plot: Despite being caught in her imaginative world, young waitress Amelie decides to help people find happiness. Her quest to spread joy leads her on a journey during which she finds true love.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Jeunet's meticulously crafted visual whimsy that rewards the big screen. The Montmartre locations and color-saturated cinematography create a theatrical experience that streaming cannot match.
Essential Wertmüller at her political and romantic peak, worth catching on the big screen. Giannini and Melato deliver career-defining performances that burn with anarchist passion and doomed love.

ratings.plot: A former high school teacher turned unionist tries to organize workers laboring with inhuman conditions at a late 19th Century textile factory.

verdict.reason: This is essential Monicelli at his most politically engaged, balancing humor with genuine working-class solidarity. Marcello Mastroianni delivers one of his finest dramatic performances as the idealistic teacher who learns that revolution requires more than good intentions.

ratings.plot: Loretta Castorini, a bookkeeper from Brooklyn, New York, finds herself in a difficult situation when she falls for the brother of the man she has agreed to marry.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Cher's Oscar-winning performance and one of the warmest romantic comedies ever made. Jewison captures the rhythms of Italian-American family life with perfect pitch and timing.

ratings.plot: Between 1968 and 1983, a San Francisco cartoonist becomes an amateur detective obsessed with tracking down the Zodiac Killer, an unidentified individual who terrorizes Northern California with a killing spree.

verdict.reason: Essential Fincher at his most methodical and unsettling. The film's forensic attention to investigative detail makes every clue feel like a breakthrough you're discovering yourself.

ratings.plot: An undercover cop infiltrates a gang of thieves who plan to rob a jewelry store.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone serious about action cinema. Ringo Lam's precise staging of the jewelry store heist and its aftermath influenced every crime thriller that followed, including Reservoir Dogs.

ratings.plot: New York actors rehearse Chekhov's "Uncle Vanya" in a dilapidated theatre.

verdict.reason: Malle's final film captures theatrical magic that streaming cannot replicate. Wallace Shawn and Andre Gregory's decade-long Chekhov workshop creates unrepeatable intimacy.

ratings.plot: Two disreputable friends get tied in with a group of criminals who turn out to be excessively violent and deceptive.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone tracking Hong Kong action cinema at its most unhinged. Ringo Lam directs with the same manic energy that made City on Fire a classic, but pushes the violence into genuinely disturbing territory.

ratings.plot: A young Shaolin follower reunites with his discouraged brothers to form a soccer team using their martial art skills to their advantage.

verdict.reason: Stephen Chow's kinetic blend of wire-fu and sports comedy deserves the big screen treatment. The digital effects that looked cheap in 2001 now play as deliberately cartoonish in the best possible way.

ratings.plot: A senior chef lives with his three grown daughters; the middle one finds her future plans affected by unexpected events and the life changes of the other household members.

verdict.reason: See this while you can catch Lee's mastery of domestic drama on a big screen. The food preparation sequences alone justify the theatrical experience, with cinematography that makes every meal a sensual event.

ratings.plot: Members of a street gang known as the Warriors must fight their way from the Bronx to their home turf on Coney Island when they are falsely accused of assassinating a respected gang leader.

verdict.reason: Essential theatrical experience for Hill's masterclass in kinetic editing and urban mythology. The film transforms New York's subway tunnels into a neon-lit gauntlet that demands the big screen treatment.

ratings.plot: Showgirls Lorelei Lee and Dorothy Shaw travel to Paris, pursued by a private detective hired by the suspicious father of Lorelei's fiancé, as well as a rich, enamored old man and many other doting admirers.

verdict.reason: Essential Hawks on a big screen where Monroe and Russell's chemistry can work its full magic. The Technicolor musical numbers demand theatrical presentation to capture their lavish spectacle.

ratings.plot: A series of comedic and nostalgic vignettes set in a 1930s Italian coastal town.

verdict.reason: Essential Fellini that demands the big screen for its carnival of memory and desire. The film's lush cinematography and intricate crowd choreography lose their magic on home viewing.

ratings.plot: Martin Eden struggles to rise above his destitute, proletarian circumstances through an intense and passionate pursuit of self-education, hoping to achieve a place among the literary elite.

verdict.reason: This is essential viewing for anyone drawn to stories of artistic ambition and class struggle. Marcello's anachronistic visual style mixes 16mm archival footage with contemporary scenes to create a temporal dreamscape that makes the 1909 Jack London novel feel urgently present.

ratings.plot: Nicky is on the run from the mob, and he turns to old pal Mikey for help.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of the most naturalistic crime films ever made. May's improvisational direction captures paranoia and friendship with a documentary intimacy that no other filmmaker has matched.

ratings.plot: An Italian Countess is allied with Nationalists during the Italian-Austrian war of unification. However, she risks betraying their cause when she falls in love with an Austrian lieutenant.

verdict.reason: One of cinema's greatest achievements in color and composition demands the biggest screen you can find. Visconti's command of Technicolor turns every frame into a painting that loses its power on anything smaller than theatrical projection.

ratings.plot: When Fatima leaves her close-knit suburban family to study philosophy in Paris, she finds herself caught between her religious upbringing and the freedom of student life in the city.

verdict.reason: Worth the trip for a fresh take on family and identity conflicts. Herzi directs with the specificity of someone who lived this exact tension between tradition and intellectual freedom.

ratings.plot: In WWII Italy, a widow and her lonely daughter seek distance between themselves and the horrors of war.

verdict.reason: Essential De Sica that deserves the big screen treatment. Loren's powerhouse performance carries one of the most harrowing wartime stories ever filmed.

ratings.plot: A movie sound recordist accidentally records the evidence that proves that a car accident was actually murder and consequently finds himself in danger.

verdict.reason: Peak De Palma paranoia thriller that demands theatrical sound design. The audio layers and split-screen work lose impact on home viewing.

ratings.plot: A tale of nineteenth-century New York high society in which a young lawyer falls in love with a woman separated from her husband, while he is engaged to the woman's cousin.

verdict.reason: Essential Scorsese on the big screen where his camera work and Dante Ferretti's production design create the suffocating world of Gilded Age Manhattan. The film's visual storytelling about repressed desire works best in a theater where every glance and gesture fills the frame.

ratings.plot: A fallen soccer superstar vows to adopt a refugee child, while becoming the naive unwitting centerpiece in in a bizarre plot to Make Portugal Great Again.

verdict.reason: One of the most genuinely bizarre films of recent years that earns every minute of its weirdness. Abrantes and Schmidt create a visual language that turns political satire into something closer to fever dream logic.

ratings.plot: Rick, a washed-up wedding singer, and Danny, a fading boy band star, bond over music and a late-night jam session. When Danny turns Rick's song into a hit, Rick sets out to reclaim the recognition he believes he deserves.

verdict.reason: Carney delivers another winning music film that earns its theatrical ticket. The director's ear for authentic musical moments and character-driven songwriting makes this worth experiencing with an audience.

ratings.plot: When Elizabeth Bennet meets the handsome Mr. Darcy, she believes he is the last man she could ever marry, but as their lives become intertwined, she finds herself captivated by the man she has sworn to hate forever.

verdict.reason: Wright's adaptation deserves the big screen treatment for its sweeping cinematography and Knightley's magnetic performance. The film transforms Austen's drawing room drama into something genuinely cinematic through its use of natural light and landscape.

ratings.plot: When two best friends develop a crush on the same boy, they develop a plan to trick him into dating them.

verdict.reason: Iwai turns a simple teenage crush into something genuinely moving about friendship and growing up. The 135-minute runtime lets the characters breathe in ways that feel completely natural rather than indulgent.

ratings.plot: Three film students vanish after traveling into a Maryland forest to film a documentary on the local Blair Witch legend, leaving only their footage behind.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for its revolutionary found footage technique that spawned countless imitators. The handheld camera work creates genuine dread through what you never see rather than cheap scares.

ratings.plot: After a deadly rodeo and a stolen kiss, Rosa and her cowboy lover flee across the Italian wilderness, pursued by Buffalo Bill and the story he refuses to rewrite.
ratings.director: Alessio Rigo de Righi, Matteo Zoppis

verdict.reason: This Italian western earns the big screen treatment with its inventive blend of rodeo spectacle and romantic fugitive drama. Directors Alessio Rigo de Righi and Matteo Zoppis craft visuals that demand theatrical viewing over streaming compression.

ratings.plot: A small-time reporter tries to convince the police that she saw a murder in the apartment across from hers.

verdict.reason: Essential early De Palma that showcases his Hitchcock obsessions before they became mannered. The split-screen sequences and Bernard Herrmann's final soundtrack create genuine theatrical tension that streaming can't match.

ratings.plot: After acknowledging his own immigrant background, Malle, tries to present the range of immigrant experiences in the US during the 1980's. In an attempt to be comprehensive, the film includes interviews with migrant workers and ill...

verdict.reason: Worth catching this rare Malle documentary that captures 1980s immigrant America with his characteristic empathy and intelligence. The 80-minute runtime keeps the sprawling subject focused without losing the human detail that makes Malle essential viewing.

ratings.plot: A research team in Antarctica is hunted by a shape-shifting alien that assumes the appearance of its victims.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Carpenter's masterclass in paranoid tension and practical effects wizardry. Rob Bottin's grotesque creature work remains unmatched in horror cinema and demands the biggest screen possible.

ratings.plot: A group of very different individuals staying at a luxurious hotel in Berlin deal with each of their respective dramas.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for the only Best Picture winner with no other Oscar nominations. Goulding's revolving door structure influenced decades of ensemble filmmaking that followed.

ratings.plot: Oilman Jan is paralyzed in an accident. His wife, who prayed for his return, feels guilty; even more, when Jan urges her to have sex with another.

verdict.reason: Essential von Trier that demands the full theatrical experience for maximum impact. Emily Watson's raw, uncompromising performance anchors one of the most emotionally brutal films ever made.

ratings.plot: While passing through a vacation resort, a newlywed couple encounters a mysterious, strikingly beautiful countess and her aide.

verdict.reason: See it for one of the most seductive vampire films ever made. Kümel's visual approach treats blood and sexuality as elegant design elements rather than exploitation.

ratings.plot: In 1924, Oskar Matzerath is born in the Free City of Danzig. At age three, he falls down a flight of stairs and stops growing. In 1939, World War II breaks out.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Schlöndorff's bold adaptation of Grass's impossible novel. The film's grotesque imagery and Bennent's fearless child performance create a theatrical experience that streaming cannot match.

ratings.plot: When his family goes away for summer vacation, a hitherto faithful husband with an overactive imagination is tempted by a sexy new neighbor.

verdict.reason: Essential Wilder that plays better with a crowd than at home. Marilyn Monroe's subway grate scene was shot on this very street corner and the film captures her at peak magnetism.

ratings.plot: A Puerto Rican former convict, just released from prison, pledges to stay away from drugs and violence despite the pressure around him and lead on to a better life outside of N.Y.C.

verdict.reason: De Palma's operatic crime saga deserves the big screen treatment for its sweeping camera work and tragic scope. Pacino delivers one of his most restrained performances as the aging gangster trying to escape his past.

ratings.plot: A mentally disturbed man takes residence in a halfway house. His mind gradually slips back into the realm created by his illness, where he replays a key part of his childhood.

verdict.reason: Worth the trip for Cronenberg's most restrained and internal work. Ralph Fiennes delivers a wordless performance that captures mental illness without spectacle or false sympathy.

ratings.plot: After a car wreck, an amnesiac woman sets out to learn what happened to her with the help of a wannabe actress.

verdict.reason: Essential Lynch on the big screen where his sound design and dream logic hit hardest. The film's fractured narrative rewards the focused attention that only a theater provides.

ratings.plot: A mysterious blonde woman kills one of the patients of a psychiatrist and then goes after the high-class hooker who witnessed the murder.

verdict.reason: Peak De Palma sleaze that belongs on the big screen where his split-screen voyeurism and museum stalking sequence can work their queasy magic. The shower scene and elevator murder are textbook examples of how to build and release tension through pure cinema.

ratings.plot: During Prohibition, Treasury agent Eliot Ness sets out to stop ruthless Chicago gangster Al Capone, and assembles a small, incorruptible team to help him.

verdict.reason: Worth seeing on the big screen for De Palma's operatic take on the gangster genre. The Odessa Steps homage alone justifies the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: A retelling of the life of the celebrated 17th-century painter through his brilliant, nearly blasphemous paintings and his flirtations with the underworld.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in how cinema can match the intensity of great painting. Jarman shoots with the same dramatic chiaroscuro lighting that made Caravaggio revolutionary, creating a visual experience that belongs on the big screen.

ratings.plot: Gianfranco Rosi's new documentary is an immersive portrait of those trying to survive in the war-torn Middle East.

verdict.reason: Rosi delivers his most powerful work yet, capturing the human cost of Middle Eastern conflict with unflinching intimacy. His patient observational style transforms 100 minutes into something close to a prayer for the displaced and forgotten.

ratings.plot: During the Vietnam War, a soldier finds himself the outsider of his own squad when they unnecessarily kidnap a female villager, for their sexual enjoyment.

verdict.reason: Essential De Palma that demands the big screen for its brutal moral clarity. The director's trademark split-screen techniques turn the Vietnam horror into a cinematic trial by fire.

ratings.plot: The lives of a disparate group of contestants intertwine in a grueling and inhumane dance marathon.

verdict.reason: One of the great American films about economic desperation that hits harder in a theater full of strangers. Pollack's relentless editing mirrors the marathon's punishing rhythm with surgical precision.

ratings.plot: On a dark, wet night a historic and regal Chinese cinema sees its final film. Together with a small handful of souls they bid "Goodbye, Dragon Inn".

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Tsai Ming-liang's meditation on cinema's death, best experienced in a theater where you become part of the ritual. The 82-minute runtime lets you sink into the hypnotic rhythms without testing your patience.

ratings.plot: Three women set out to find eligible millionaires to marry, but find true love in the process.

verdict.reason: See this CinemaScope pioneer on the biggest screen you can find. The wide-format photography that defined 1950s moviegoing deserves proper theatrical presentation.

ratings.plot: A laconic, chain-smoking barber blackmails his wife's boss and lover for money to invest in dry cleaning, but his plan goes terribly wrong.

verdict.reason: The Coens' noir masterpiece deserves the theatrical treatment for Roger Deakins' black-and-white cinematography alone. Every shadow and cigarette wisp was designed for the big screen experience.

ratings.plot: The life of brilliant but tortured artist Vincent van Gogh.

verdict.reason: Douglas delivers one of his most committed performances as van Gogh in this rare Minnelli biographical drama. The Technicolor cinematography transforms the artist's paintings into living cinema that belongs on a big screen.

ratings.plot: A woman from the lower class desperately tries to get her daughter into the movies.

verdict.reason: Essential Visconti that deserves the big screen treatment. Anna Magnani delivers one of her most ferocious performances as a mother whose ambition borders on madness.

ratings.plot: An adaptation of nine stories from Boccaccio's "Decameron".

verdict.reason: Pasolini's bawdy medieval tales earn their theatrical stripes through sheer cinematic audacity. The director's earthy humanism and period authenticity create a feast for the senses that streaming diminishes.

ratings.plot: Mark marries Marnie although she is a habitual thief and has serious psychological problems, and tries to help her confront and resolve them.

verdict.reason: One of Hitchcock's most psychologically complex films deserves the big screen treatment. The director's use of rear projection and color creates a deliberately artificial world that amplifies Marnie's fractured mental state.

ratings.plot: As two couples are visiting Niagara Falls, tensions between one wife and her husband reach the level of murder.

verdict.reason: Catch this Technicolor showcase that uses the Falls as more than scenery. Hathaway turns the tourist destination into a character that amplifies the noir tension.

ratings.plot: A young actor's obsession with spying on a beautiful woman who lives nearby leads to a baffling series of events with drastic consequences.

verdict.reason: One of De Palma's most unhinged Hitchcock riffs deserves the big screen treatment. The telescope voyeurism and split-diopter shots work best when projected large enough to make you complicit in the protagonist's obsession.

Essential viewing for anyone serious about cinema's most visionary directors. Fellini's hallucinogenic production design and carnival atmosphere create a theatrical experience that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: Police Inspector Pao is trying to catch Mak Kwan, a gang member who is first arrested, but then escapes from the prison. By chance, Pao realizes that the target of Kwan's gang is the H. K. Jockey Club.

verdict.reason: Ringo Lam delivers one of the tightest police procedurals of the 1990s Hong Kong wave. The cat-and-mouse structure builds to a finale that justifies the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: Desolate by the loss of his daughter, a geneticist creates a monstrous new mutation.
ratings.director: Kazuki Ômori, Kôji Hashimoto, Kenjirô Ohmori

verdict.reason: The most beautiful monster movie ever made deserves the biggest screen possible. The practical effects and botanical horror imagery create visuals that streaming cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: Whilst writing a children's book, a woman interrupted by images unsure if they may, or may not be real

verdict.reason: Essential Altman from his peak period that deserves the big screen treatment. The film's hallucinatory sound design by Stomu Yamashta creates an unsettling psychological landscape that home viewing cannot replicate.

ratings.plot: While recovering in Venice, sickly composer Gustav von Aschenbach becomes dangerously fixated with teenager Tadzio.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Visconti's mastery of decay and desire rendered through impossibly lush cinematography. The Mann adaptation transforms literary obsession into pure visual poetry that demands the big screen.

ratings.plot: This ultra-hip, post-modern vampire tale is set in contemporary New York City. Members of a dysfunctional family of vampires are trying to come to terms with each other, in the wake of their father's death. Meanwhile, they are bei...

verdict.reason: A singular vampire film that earns its cult status through Almereyda's hypnotic blend of 35mm and Pixelvision. The new 4K restoration of the director's cut makes this the definitive way to see one of the 90s' most inventive horror films.

ratings.plot: A toxic, ever-evolving alien life-form from the Dark Gaseous Nebula arrives to consume rampant pollution, and neither humanity nor Godzilla may be able to stop it.

verdict.reason: This is the strangest entry in the Godzilla series and worth seeing for its psychedelic weirdness alone. Banno turns kaiju destruction into an anti-pollution fever dream with animated sequences and nightclub musical numbers that feel completely unhinged.

ratings.plot: An American agent, under false suspicion of disloyalty, must discover and expose the real spy without the help of his organization.

verdict.reason: De Palma's split-screen compositions and the Langley vault sequence demand theatrical viewing. The practical stunts and paranoid atmosphere lose impact on smaller screens.

ratings.plot: Mae Doyle comes back to her hometown a cynical woman. Her brother Joe fears that his love, fish cannery worker Peggy, may wind up like Mae. Mae marries Jerry and has a baby; she is happy but restless, drawn to Jerry's friend Earl.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Lang completists and anyone interested in his American noir period. Barbara Stanwyck delivers one of her most complex performances as a woman caught between desire and domesticity.

ratings.plot: The titular river unites a farmer recently released from prison, his young son and an ambitious saloon singer. In order to survive, each must be purged of anger, and each must learn to understand and care for the others.

verdict.reason: Worth the trip for one of the most beautiful color westerns ever shot. Preminger turns the CinemaScope wilderness into a character that dwarfs Mitchum and Monroe's considerable screen presence.

ratings.plot: A father's wish magically brings a wooden boy to life in Italy, giving him a chance to care for the child.

verdict.reason: This is del Toro's most personal work, animated with handcrafted stop-motion that demands the big screen. The wartime setting and puppet craftsmanship create a visual experience that streaming cannot match.

ratings.plot: A mysterious young drifter who calls herself Nomi Malone hitches a ride to Las Vegas, where she finds work as a stripper and sets about clawing her way to the top of the Vegas showgirls.

verdict.reason: Verhoeven's masterpiece of American excess disguised as exploitation trash. The theatrical experience amplifies both the absurdity and the genuine critique of Vegas capitalism.

ratings.plot: In spring, a young girl leaves the island of Hokkaido to attend university in Tokyo.

verdict.reason: Iwai captures the quiet poetry of transition in just 67 minutes without wasting a frame. The film distills longing into pure cinema through his signature use of color and light.

movies.title: Bumming in Beijing: The Last Dreamers

ratings.plot: Independent Chinese documentary by Wu Wenguan following and interviewing a group of young artists around the time of the events Tiananmen about their lives and careers.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for its raw documentation of Beijing's underground art scene in the immediate aftermath of Tiananmen. Wu Wenguang's handheld camera captures intimate conversations that feel impossibly honest given the political climate.

ratings.plot: Police gets a call-out to a lonely hotel in the Alps. When an officer gets to the hotel everything seems to be all right. Suddenly, an avalanche cuts them out from the rest of the world, and strange things are going to happen.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in Soviet-era genre filmmaking that predates Western sci-fi thrillers by years. Kromanov's atmospheric direction transforms a remote Alpine hotel into a genuinely unsettling psychological maze.

ratings.plot: After losing her fiance in a fatal mountain-climbing incident, Hiroko was devastated. She comes across his childhood address in a school yearbook and impulsively writes to him. But what happens when she receives a reply?

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone seeking Japanese cinema at its most emotionally precise. Iwai's snow-covered cinematography turns grief into something achingly beautiful without ever feeling manipulative.

ratings.plot: A young schoolgirl gets involved in the triads of Hong Kong after a classmate is killed in a fight.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in Hong Kong cinema's golden age. Ringo Lam's brutal direction strips away any romanticism from triad life with unflinching violence that feels genuinely dangerous.

ratings.plot: During a five year period an Italian filmmaker documents the world of down-on-their-luck individuals who live in a Californian desert trying to get by one day at a time. None of them has more than a vehicle, a dog and some clothes.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in observational documentary at its purest. Rosi spent five years gaining access to this invisible community, and the patience shows in every frame.
Essential Cronenberg on 35mm at a venue that programs thoughtfully. The body horror imagery hits harder on the big screen than any home format can deliver.

movies.title: Grand Budapest Hotel preceded by Hotel Chevalier

verdict.reason: See Anderson's masterpiece on the big screen with the rare theatrical pairing of his Darjeeling Limited short. The double feature creates a perfect evening of his signature dollhouse aesthetic at proper cinema scale.
One of Chantal Akerman's essential films deserves the big screen treatment. The lengthy takes and Aurore Clément's restrained performance create a hypnotic rhythm that streaming cannot replicate.

movies.title: The Goalie's Anxiety at the Penalty Kick

ratings.plot: Goalkeeper Josef Bloch is ejected during a game for foul play. He leaves the field and goes to spend the night with a cinema cashier.

verdict.reason: Essential early Wenders that shows his mastery of alienation through precise framing and sound design. The film moves with the deliberate rhythm of someone losing their grip on reality, making every mundane interaction feel charged with menace.

movies.title: This Long Century presents Ben Rivers

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for anyone interested in experimental cinema's relationship to landscape and time. Rivers' 16mm work transforms ordinary rural spaces into something approaching the mythic through patient, static compositions that reward theatrical attention.
Essential viewing for Herzog's hypnotic voice guiding you through humanity's earliest art. The 3D format transforms the cave walls into a living timeline that no home screen can replicate.

ratings.plot: A black-and-white love letter to pre-gentrification New York City, Phil Hartman's NO PICNIC captures a remote time and place - the East Village circa 1985, a vibrant, seedy neighborhood populated by musicians, pimps and poets. Mac...

verdict.reason: Phil Hartman's time capsule captures 1985 East Village before gentrification erased it forever. The black-and-white cinematography and authentic street-level energy make this a rare document worth seeing properly projected.

ratings.plot: If you found out we weren't alone, if someone showed you, proved it to you, would that frighten you?

verdict.reason: Spielberg tackling alien contact is worth the trip for any serious moviegoer. The director's mastery of wonder and dread makes this the rare sci-fi thriller that demands the big screen experience.

ratings.plot: On the Italian/Austrian front during World War I, a disastrous Italian attack upon the Austrian positions leads to a mutiny among the Italian troops.

verdict.reason: Essential Rosi at his most politically sharp, dismantling military hierarchy with surgical precision. The courtroom sequences burn with the director's signature blend of procedural realism and moral outrage.

movies.title: But I'm a Cheerleader Movie Party

ratings.plot: Megan (Natasha Lyonne) considers herself a typical American girl. She excels in school and cheerleading and she has a handsome football-playing boyfriend, even though she isn't that crazy about him. So she's stunned when her parents decide she's gay and send her to True Directions, a boot camp meant to alter her sexual orientation. While there, Megan meets a rebellious and unashamed teen lesbian, Graham (Clea DuVall). Though Megan still feels confused, she starts to have feelings for Graham. Also stars Michelle Williams, RuPaul, Bud Cort, Mink Stole, and Melanie Lynskey.

verdict.reason: This cult comedy holds up as sharp satire and deserves to be seen with a crowd that gets the jokes. Babbit's candy-colored production design creates a visual feast that rewards the big screen experience.

ratings.plot: Director Robert Zemeckis' deliciously twisted black comedy begins with Broadway has-been Meryl Streep bulldozing her way through a memorably misguided disco number and Goldie Hawn gorging herself on canned cake frosting – and things only get weirder and wilder from there. DEATH BECOMES HER offers up a classic catfight unlike anything you've ever seen, as terminally vain Madeline Ashton (Streep) clashes with her lifelong rival, beauty expert Helen Sharp (Hawn), in a perilous quest for eternal youth and glamour. Caught in the middle are plastic surgeon Ernest Menville (Bruce Willis) and the mysterious goddess-y Lisle Von Rhuman (Isabella Rossellini), who peddles an ancient elixir that provides "a touch of magic in this world obsessed with science." We've often heard "beauty is pain," and Madeline and Helen take that old adage to hair-raising new extremes. DEATH BECOMES HER deservedly won the best visual effects Oscar in 1993. But the eye-popping digital wizardry doesn't eclipse sensational comic performances from Streep, Hawn, and Willis, who create a hilariously offbeat love triangle under Zemeckis' stylish direction, and Alan Silvestri's urgently over-the-top score is the perfect frosting on this bittersweet cake. Yes, these are the moments that make life worth living...

verdict.reason: One of the great dark comedies about vanity and mortality, best experienced with a crowd that will gasp at every twisted visual effect. Zemeckis created digital wizardry that still looks disturbing thirty years later, and Streep and Hawn commit fully to the camp.

ratings.plot: One of the most derided movies of the 20th century is also one of its most spectacularly misunderstood. The odd-couple tag team of Dutch provocateur director Paul Verhoeven (ROBOCOP, ELLE) and Sleazoid-Hungarian-American writer Joe Eszterhas (FLASHDANCE, SLIVER) first came together with 1992's BASIC INSTINCT, and everyone, including the financial backers and the producer/distributors of this movie, assumed lightning would strike twice for the duo with 1995's SHOWGIRLS. Universally panned by critics upon its initial release, people found the multi-tap combo of inane dialogue, wild over-acting, and extreme obscenity and grotesquerie to be just too much. Then the "so-bad-it's-good" crowd sunk their hollow claws into the movie, claiming it as one of the "best worst" movies of all time. To be sure, yes, this is a movie in which Saved By the Bell 's Elizabeth Berkley sets fire to her TV-star image by grinding her pelvis on Kyle MacLachlan's lap until he simulates sexual climax while Gina Gershon watches. She shoves chips into her mouth and reminisces about her days of eating and enjoying "puppy chow," between bouts of trying to make it as a Las Vegas showgirl "goddess." The assumption that critics and chuckleheads alike have made are that a) Verhoeven and Eszterhas weren't trying to make something with humor (they were), and b) that the surface story is meant to be taken at face value (it's not). Verhoemie don't play that. SHOWGIRLS is a complicated, satirical masterpiece that tests the audience's limits of empathy and challenges some basic assumptions about American dreams of celebrity, pokes at the artificiality of Hollywood glitz and glamour, and shines a light on the misogyny and dehumanization inherent in the entertainment industry, all while keeping the audiences on their toes as they try to know whether they should laugh, cry, or scratch their heads because there are random interruptions by chimpanzees and the fiercest dancing ever committed to celluloid. (Laird Jimenez)

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Verhoeven's most misunderstood satire, best experienced with a crowd that gets the joke. The communal energy transforms what many dismiss as camp into the sharp cultural critique it actually is.

ratings.plot: Danny Boyle's explosive film tracks the misadventures of young men in Edinburgh trying to find their way out of joblessness, aimless relationships, and drug addiction. Some are successful, while others hopelessly are not. Based on Irvine Walsh's novel, TRAINSPOTTING melds grit with poetry, resulting in a film of harsh truths and stunning grace.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for Boyle's kinetic direction and the Choose Life monologue that still hits like a freight train. The 35mm print makes Underworld's techno soundtrack feel like it's pumping through your veins.

ratings.plot: Rio de Janeiro’s criminal underworld is run by an unexpected boss: Diaba (Milton Gonçalves), a femme queen with a taste for power and violence. When the police come after her boy toy, Diaba attempts to find a scapegoat to take the blame and a plot to dethrone Her Majesty arises! Always clad in gloriously colorful outfits and accessories inspired by Afro-Brazilian culture, Diaba runs a mob of eccentric misfits made up of drag queens, pimps, prostitutes, and queer folks of all stripes. The movie’s bold and audacious color palette and camp aesthetic evokes the early films of Pedro Almodóvar, while its stylized and over-the-top violence is giving Quentin Tarantino queer fever dream.

verdict.reason: This Brazilian queer crime film delivers the theatrical spectacle that streaming can't match. Milton Gonçalves commands the screen in drag as a crime boss whose Afro-Brazilian costume design alone justifies the big screen experience.

ratings.plot: Presented in Spanish with English subtitles Mexico City. A fatal car crash. Three lives collide. A teenager risks it all to run away with his brother’s wife. A model loses everything after moving in with her lover. A homeless man unearths the ghosts of his past. As their stories unfold, human chaos, cruelty, and tenderness come to light – revealing they are more connected than they seem.

verdict.reason: Essential viewing for one of the most visceral debuts in modern cinema. Iñárritu's breakneck editing and raw Mexico City locations demand the theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: About CHIME 2024 / 45 min. A masterclass in escalating dread and shocking violence, CHIME reaffirms Kiyoshi Kurosawa as one of modern horror’s most innovative and unpredictable visionaries. During a class, culinary instructor Matsuoka (Mutsuo Yoshioka) witnesses the suicide of a young student (Seiichi Kohinata), driven to insanity by what he claims is a chiming sound that controls his mind. Soon, Matsuoka begins hearing it too, and descends into a mental abyss that warps his perception of reality and gives vent to his darkest impulses. Expertly blending psychological portraiture and hallucinatory mystery, Kurosawa offers a chilling depiction of madness that interrogates the very stability of our everyday existence, with the director’s patented creeping tracking shots and complex sound design fashioning an immersively terrifying and unnerving cinematic experience. About SERPENT'S PATH 1998 / 85 min. Straight off CURE (1997), his international breakthrough, Kiyoshi Kurosawa directed two low-budget films using the same basic premise and the same lead actor (Sho Aikawa) to completely different ends. The experiment first resulted in SERPENT'S PATH (1998, later remade in 2024), a dark gangland thriller with philosophical overtones. Obsessed with avenging his young daughter’s murder, yakuza subordinate Miyashita (Teruyuki Kagawa) recruits Nijima (Aikawa), a brilliant yet strangely detached math teacher, to help carry out a scheme to kidnap and torture the man allegedly responsible. But the plan goes awry when their target, Otsuki (Yurei Yanagi), fingers another mobster as the mastermind behind Miyashita’s tragedy. As the two partners ascend the yakuza chain of command in search of the true culprit, Miyashita and Nijima follow the cold, calculating logic of revenge, descending into a moral abyss from which they may never surface. Featuring Kurosawa’s patented long takes and his claustrophobic arrangement of space, SERPENT'S PATH is one of the legendary director’s most chilling investigations into the endless cycle of violence and the evil that lodges in every heart.

verdict.reason: Essential theatrical pairing that shows Kurosawa's range from his latest horror mastery to a foundational yakuza thriller. The double feature format lets you trace the evolution of his signature tracking shots and sound design across 26 years of filmmaking.

ratings.plot: A reporter's personal journey and major news coverage intersect in this film debuting at DC/Dox, emphasizing journalism's vital role in revealing truth during an era when independent reporting faces challenges.

verdict.reason: Skip this documentary about Hollywood's idea theft problem unless you work in the industry. The subject matter feels more suited to a trade publication article than a theatrical experience.

ratings.plot: The children of a once famous artist hire a forger to complete some unfinished, long ago abandoned canvases so they'll have an inheritance when he dies.

verdict.reason: Skip this one unless you have a personal connection to the subject matter. The execution feels more like a well-intentioned documentary than essential cinema.

ratings.plot: After breaking the mysterious "One Wish Willow" to win his crush's heart, a hopeless romantic finds himself getting exactly what he asked for but soon discovers that some desires come at a dark, sinister price.

verdict.reason: Generic horror premise dressed up with high production values that cannot mask thin storytelling. Barker's direction lacks the innovation needed to elevate familiar wish-fulfillment scares.

ratings.plot: A supermom lands a Shark Tank deal, switching roles with her breadwinner husband. He struggles to adapt as a stay-at-home dad to their three daughters.

verdict.reason: Generic role-reversal comedy relies on tired gender stereotypes without fresh insight. Eric Appel brings no distinctive voice to well-worn family dysfunction material.
Skip this one unless you're desperate for underground cinema tonight. The experimental approach never coheres into anything worth the trip across town.

ratings.plot: Every night a shepherd reads aloud a murder mystery, pretending his sheep can understand. When he is found dead, the sheep realize at once that it was a murder and think they know everything about how to go about solving it.

verdict.reason: Illumination-style animation wrapped around a premise too cute for its own good. Balda's direction feels assembly-line despite the quirky murder mystery angle.

ratings.plot: In 1930s Algeria, the daily life of an indifferent Frenchman is shaken by the death of his mother and a fateful encounter on a beach.

verdict.reason: Skip this generic thriller that wastes a solid setup. Prasad never builds real suspense beyond the obvious ride-share paranoia.

ratings.plot: This contemporary romantic comedy based on a global bestseller follows native New Yorker Rachel Chu to Singapore to meet her boyfriend's family.

verdict.reason: Skip unless you need the communal rom-com experience tonight. The Singapore wedding sequence is the only part that truly benefits from theatrical viewing.

ratings.plot: After a young man gets separated from his friends in the woods, he falls in to a 10 foot deep pit of spikes, impaling him through his leg and leaving him trapped. He quickly learns that his fall was not an accident.

verdict.reason: Skip this derivative survival horror that offers nothing new to the trapped-in-a-hole subgenre. The premise exhausts itself in the first act and never recovers.
Too little information to justify the subway fare when better options exist tonight. Without knowing the director, year, or venue, the gamble isn't worth the risk.

ratings.plot: A White couple's attitudes are challenged when their daughter introduces them to her Black fiancé.

verdict.reason: This Oscar winner feels more like a liberal lecture than a movie. The social relevance that once made it essential now exposes how cautiously it approached its own subject.

ratings.plot: A year after disposing of the body of a man they accidentally killed, a group of dumb teenagers are stalked by a bumbling serial killer.

verdict.reason: Skip this tired franchise revival that mistakes volume for humor. The Wayans brothers' absence strips away the original's sharper pop culture timing.

ratings.plot: A young Russian filmmaker becomes an unlikely advisor to Vladimir Putin as he rises to power in post-Soviet Russia, navigating the new era's complexities and chaos.

verdict.reason: Skip this muddled political drama that fails to make its Russian power games compelling. The narrative never finds a clear perspective on its ambitious subject matter.

ratings.plot: Mario ventures into space, exploring cosmic worlds and tackling galactic challenges far from the familiar Mushroom Kingdom.
ratings.director: Aaron Horvath, Michael Jelenic, Pierre Leduc

verdict.reason: Another disposable Nintendo cash grab with nothing to justify the theater trip. The cosmic setting feels like a cheap excuse to recycle familiar beats from the first film.

ratings.plot: The early life of musician Michael Jackson, from the discovery of his talent as the lead of the Jackson Five to the artist whose creative ambition fueled a pursuit to become the biggest entertainer in the world.

verdict.reason: Skip this sanitized biopic that treats Jackson like a product to be marketed. Fuqua's direction lacks the complexity needed to handle such a controversial figure.

ratings.plot: With Italy about to go to war in 1914, this is the story of the encounter between a goatherd called Lucia, the commune of young Northern Europeans led by Seybu and the town's young doctor on the unique island of Capri.

verdict.reason: Skip this unfocused period piece that wastes its stunning Mediterranean setting. Martone never finds a clear dramatic through-line in the bloated 122-minute runtime.

ratings.plot: Jigsaw abducts a doctor in order to keep himself alive while he watches his new apprentice put an unlucky citizen named Jeff through a brutal test.

verdict.reason: Skip this one unless you need to complete the franchise marathon. Bousman cranks the gore to punishing levels without the tight plotting that made the original work.

ratings.plot: A young man on Earth discovers a fabulous secret legacy as the prince of an alien planet, and must recover a magic sword and return home to protect his kingdom.

verdict.reason: Skip this bloated franchise resurrection that mistakes runtime for epic scope. Even Idris Elba as Man-At-Arms cannot save the film from Jared Leto's scenery-chewing Skeletor.
Skip this one unless you have a specific interest in Czech political cinema from the 1960s. The film's historical importance doesn't translate to compelling viewing for modern audiences.

movies.title: PRIVATE EVENT TODAY IN THEATER & COMMISSARY

verdict.reason: No public screening means no ticket to buy. Check back tomorrow for regular programming.

ratings.plot: A struggling Hollywood writer accepts an invitaion to dinner witha powerful producer. Somewhere between the hors d'oeuvres and dessert he has to make the pitch of his life. But how far will he go to satisfy Andre's hunger for a ju...

verdict.reason: Skip this forgettable short that trades on a famous title for cheap recognition. The premise about a Hollywood pitch meeting lacks the wit or insight to justify even a brief runtime.
Skip this one unless you have a specific interest in contemporary Brazilian cinema. The execution feels more like a film school exercise than a fully realized feature.

ratings.plot: A stand-up comedy special from Akaash Singh, known for his work on the Flagrant podcast.

verdict.reason: Skip this overlong comedy special that stretches thin material to feature length. Singh works better in podcast format where his timing feels more natural.

ratings.plot: Nashville singer Elizabeth Cook embarks on a journey to break free from country music constraints, embracing her authentic artistic self-expression.

verdict.reason: Skip this one unless you need to kill 90 minutes with something forgettable. The premise never develops beyond its initial setup and the execution feels more like a rough draft than a finished film.

ratings.plot: Twenty-six years after outrunning a suspiciously familiar masked killer (“Ghostface”), the Core Four are back in the killer’s crosshairs, and no horror movie IP is safe. Marlon Wayans (“Shorty”), Shawn Wayans (“Ray”), Anna Faris (“Cindy”), and Regina Hall (“Brenda”) reunite in SCARY MOVIE (2026) alongside returning favorites and fresh faces to slash through reboots, remakes, requels, prequels, sequels, spin-offs, elevated horror, origin stories, anything with the word legacy in it, and every “final chapter” that absolutely isn’t final. Nothing is sacred. No trope survives. Every line gets crossed. The Wayans are back to cancel the Cancel Culture.

verdict.reason: Skip this desperate franchise resurrection that arrives two decades too late. The original cast reunion cannot mask the fact that horror parody peaked when these films still felt fresh.

movies.title: Field of Dreams + His Own Way: The Eddie Russell Story

ratings.plot: Following close on the heels of BULL DURHAM, star Kevin Costner hit a home run with 1989’s FIELD OF DREAMS, an entirely different kind of baseball movie that immediately put "If you build it, they will come" into the catalog of American catchphrases. Based on W.P. Kinsella's novel "Shoeless Joe," FIELD OF DREAMS is a singular mix of fantasy, comedy, baseball history, and sentimentality – a movie rather out of step with the cinema of the era. But audiences immediately latched onto the story of Ray (Costner), an Iowa farmer who is spooked by ethereal voices in his cornfield and decides to build a baseball diamond in his backyard. Neighbors and family members think he's lost his mind, until – well, we won't spoil the surprises here. Costner is superbly supported by screen legends like Burt Lancaster and James Earl Jones, as well as the endearingly feisty Amy Madigan, Ray Liotta, Timothy Busfield, and Frank Whaley (who was in just about everything in the late '80s). Like a sorcerer, director and screenwriter Phil Alden Robinson combined the various elements of the tale into an absolutely captivating experience. Fair warning: Even the toughest tough guys have been known to completely lose it midway through this one, so prepare accordingly.

movies.title: BTS World Tour 'ARIRANG' in Busan: Live Viewing

ratings.plot: The historic journey continues. Following the record-breaking opening of the ‘ARIRANG’ world tour, pop royalty BTS returns to the iconic Busan Asiad Main Stadium for a monumental homecoming, broadcast live to cinemas around the world. This marks their poignant return to the very venue where they shared their final full-group performance before enlistment in 3 years and 8 months. Spanning 34 cities with 85 shows, this tour sets a new pinnacle as the most extensive tour ever by a Korean artist. Most notably, the performance falls on June 13 – BTS’s debut anniversary – adding a profound layer of symbolism as the group celebrates their legacy and future. Titled ARIRANG, the tour accompanies BTS’s fifth full-length album, weaving together the group’s identity through honest introspection and the universal themes of longing and deep love. With its signature spectacular 360-degree in-the-round stage design, the show delivers an immersive experience that places you at the heart of the celebration. Don’t miss this defining cultural moment. Share the electrifying energy of BTS’ stage on the big screen worldwide on a special day, June 13, from Busan.

verdict.reason: Worth it if you need the communal energy of a live broadcast with fellow fans. The 195-minute runtime and cinema viewing format make this more of an endurance test than an essential theatrical experience.

movies.title: Double Feature: Blues in The Night & Black Angel

ratings.plot:
Double Feature!


BLUES IN THE NIGHT

The first noir film to delve into the jazz world, Blues in the Night details the rise and inevitable fall of a St. Louis band. They land their big break when they get booked at a New Jersey nightclub, but the band’s leader, played by Richard Whorf, falls under the spell of the club’s femme fatale owner. It doesn’t take long for the other members to sour, setting off a chain reaction of jealousy, betrayal, and, eventually, murder. The supporting cast is superb, made up of familiar noir faces like Jack Carson, Howard Da Silva and Lloyd Nolan, while the screenplay by Robert Rossen makes good on the lyrics of the tragic, Oscar®-nominated title song. Future director Elia Kazan has his biggest movie role as the band’s clarinetist.



BLACK ANGEL

Kirk Bennett is sentenced to die for the murder of gorgeous actress Mavis Marlowe (Constance Dowling), but his wife (June Vincent) won’t give up trying to prove his innocence. She desperately enlists the aid of Marty Blair (Dan Duryea), a pickled pianist on the skids who happens to be the victim’s husband! The amateur sleuths go undercover as a performing duo, tracing clues to a ritzy Sunset Strip nightclub run by shady operator Marko (Peter Lorre). Bringing extra heat is Broderick Crawford as a dogged LAPD police captain. As usual in Woolrich stories, things aren’t what they seem—leading to one of the writer’s most memorable and unexpected climaxes. Known for his smarmy, villainous turns in many films of the period, here Duryea had his first role as a leading man in one of the era’s best adaptations of a novel by Cornell Woolrich, the undisputed master of noir fiction. Featuring fantastic art direction by Martin Obzina and inventive camerawork by Paul Ivano.
This movie, based on William Shakespeare's Othello, is neatly positioned as a vehicle to showcase some of the best jazz musicians of the period, including Dave Brubeck and Charles Mingus.
